issue: Correct option arranges renal US during admission for a TYPICAL first UTI in a 5-month-old with normal antenatal scans. Stem doesn't establish atypical features; RCH does not mandate US during admission for typical first UTI in this age group when antenatal scans are normal.
rch_says: Renal US during acute illness reserved for atypical UTI. Infants <6 months without antenatal monitoring or with recurrent UTI: US. With normal antenatal scans and typical first UTI: no routine US.
proposed_fix: Clarify in stem whether antenatal monitoring was complete and whether atypical features are present, then make correct option 'no further imaging' if typical or 'renal US' if atypical/no antenatal scans. As written it overstates US indication.

issue: Correct option = 'Renal tract ultrasound within 2 weeks'. After a typical first UTI in a 6-month-old with no atypical features and normal response, RCH does not routinely require US. Stem doesn't establish atypical UTI or absent antenatal monitoring.
rch_says: Most children do not require US after first UTI; US reserved for atypical or recurrent UTI, or <6 months with no antenatal monitoring or recurrent infection.
proposed_fix: Either add atypical feature or absent antenatal monitoring to justify US, or change correct option to 'no routine imaging'. Also note empiric amoxicillin-clavulanate is not the RCH first-line; RCH lists cefalexin (cystitis) or gentamicin (IV).

issue: Correct option starts oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole empirically. RCH first-line empirical oral for paediatric UTI is cefalexin, not trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. Trimethoprim is acceptable when sensitivities known but is not the RCH empirical recommendation.
rch_says: Empiric oral cystitis: cefalexin 20 mg/kg (max 750 mg) TDS. Empiric oral pyelonephritis: cefalexin 25 mg/kg QID (<12 mo) or 45 mg/kg TDS (>=12 mo). Trimethoprim only after sensitivities.
proposed_fix: Change correct option to 'Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg/dose TDS for 7 days while awaiting culture; review at end of course'. Demote trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole to a distractor noting it is reasonable only once sensitivities return.

issue: Correct option 'cefalexin 25 mg/kg/dose TWICE daily for 7 days'. RCH cefalexin dosing for pyelonephritis in >=12 months is 45 mg/kg TDS; for cystitis 20 mg/kg TDS. Twice-daily cefalexin is not an RCH-aligned regimen.
rch_says: Cefalexin TDS for cystitis (20 mg/kg) or TDS/QID for pyelonephritis (25 mg/kg QID <12mo; 45 mg/kg TDS >=12mo).
proposed_fix: Change dosing frequency to TDS (three-times daily) per RCH. Choose dose per cystitis vs pyelonephritis framing.

issue: Correct option targets SpO2 'at least 92%'. RCH Bronchiolitis CPG explicitly targets 90% (not 92%); brief desats <90% alone not an indication to start oxygen. The 92% target is from older NICE/AAP and is more aggressive than RCH.
rch_says: Target SpO2 is 90% unless underlying cardiopulmonary disease. Brief desaturations <90% alone are not an indication to start supplemental oxygen.
proposed_fix: Change SpO2 target from 92% to 90% per RCH. Distinguish in rationale that 92% is NICE/older AAP, not RCH.

issue: Correct option targets SpO2 92-96% for bronchiolitis. RCH targets 90%; the 92-96% band is a NICE/UK frame.
rch_says: Target SpO2 90%; brief dips below 90% alone do not warrant oxygen.
proposed_fix: Change SpO2 target to 'maintain SpO2 at least 90%' per RCH.

issue: SpO2 target '92 percent or above'. RCH = 90%.
rch_says: Target SpO2 90% in bronchiolitis.
proposed_fix: Change target from 92% to 90% per RCH.

issue: Correct option uses '92% on room air' as admission threshold. RCH would use 90% target. The 92% threshold over-admits per RCH and is NICE-influenced.
rch_says: RCH admission criteria are clinical (apnoea, prematurity <37/40, age <10 weeks, comorbidity, indigenous ethnicity, growth restriction, feeding <50%) plus inability to maintain SpO2 90% on room air, not 92%.
proposed_fix: Change SpO2 admit threshold from 92% to 90% per RCH and add the RCH risk factors as alternative admit triggers.

issue: Correct option admits for 'nasal high-flow oxygen'. RCH would use low-flow first; HFNC reserved for low-flow failure. Stem doesn't establish low-flow trial.
rch_says: Standard low-flow nasal prong O2 first-line; HFNC reserved for low-flow failure.
proposed_fix: Change to 'low-flow nasal prong O2 with NG feeds and observation', or add a failed-low-flow precondition to the stem.

issue: Correct option says 'observe for at least 4 hours' after nebulised adrenaline. RCH = 3 hours.
rch_says: 3-hour observation after nebulised adrenaline.
proposed_fix: Change to '3 hours' per RCH.

issue: 'Minimum 4 hour observation' post nebulised adrenaline. RCH = 3 hours.
rch_says: 3-hour post-adrenaline observation.
proposed_fix: Change '4 hours' to '3 hours' per RCH.

issue: 'Observe for at least 4 hours' post-adrenaline. RCH = 3 hours.
rch_says: 3-hour observation post nebulised adrenaline.
proposed_fix: Change '4 hours' to '3 hours' per RCH.

issue: Correct option = oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg + 'observe in ED for 4 hours' (no adrenaline given). RCH does not require fixed 4-hour observation post-dex alone. Also 0.6 mg/kg is the RCH severe-croup dose; for this stem (mild-moderate features) RCH recommends 0.15 mg/kg.
rch_says: Dex 0.15 mg/kg for mild/moderate; 0.6 mg/kg only for severe/life-threatening. Post-dex observation tied to clinical resolution, not a fixed 4-hour clock.
proposed_fix: Change dexamethasone dose to 0.15 mg/kg (or relabel stem severity as severe to match 0.6 mg/kg). Reduce 4-hour fixed wait to clinical-resolution criterion.

issue: 'Minimum 4 hours after the last adrenaline dose' - RCH says 3 hours.
rch_says: 3-hour observation post nebulised adrenaline.
proposed_fix: Change '4 hours' to '3 hours' per RCH.

issue: Correct option administers BOTH oral dex 0.6 mg/kg AND nebulised adrenaline. Stem describes a child who is 'alert, drinking small sips, pinker after being upset' - this reads as moderate not severe. RCH reserves combined dex 0.6 mg/kg + nebulised adrenaline for severe/life-threatening croup, and 0.15 mg/kg dex alone for mild/moderate.
rch_says: Mild/moderate: dex 0.15 mg/kg PO alone. Severe/life-threatening: dex 0.6 mg/kg (max 12 mg) plus nebulised adrenaline.
proposed_fix: Either upgrade the stem severity (stridor at rest, persistent recessions) to justify 0.6 mg/kg + adrenaline, or change correct option to 'oral dex 0.15 mg/kg alone'.

issue: Correct option observes 'at least 30 minutes' post oral dexamethasone. Dexamethasone peak effect is around 1-2 hours; 30 minutes is too short to demonstrate response. If stridor at rest persists, observation should continue until that settles.
rch_says: Observation should extend until clinical improvement in stridor at rest; dexamethasone peak effect 1-2 hours, not 30 minutes.
proposed_fix: Change '30 minutes' to 'observe until stridor at rest settles (typically 1-2 hours)'.

issue: Correct option observes 'at least 30 minutes' before discharge. Inadequate for stridor-at-rest croup; dex effect peaks at 1-2 hours.
rch_says: Observe until stridor at rest settles; dex effect 1-2 hours.
proposed_fix: Extend observation to clinical resolution of stridor at rest (~1-2 hours).

issue: Correct option specifies 'observe at least 4 hours'. RCH = 3 hours post-adrenaline. The 8 mg flat dose is reasonable for a ~12-13 kg 2-year-old at 0.6 mg/kg (~7-8 mg); within RCH range.
rch_says: 3-hour observation post-adrenaline.
proposed_fix: Change '4 hours' to '3 hours' per RCH.

issue: Correct option commences IV vasopressin infusion. RCH refractory anaphylaxis algorithm does not specifically recommend vasopressin; it lists IV adrenaline infusion titration, plus glucagon for beta-blocker patients. Vasopressin appears in some intensive-care rescue protocols but is not the RCH-cited next step.
rch_says: RCH refractory anaphylaxis: titrate IV adrenaline infusion, ensure airway/breathing, fluids, consider glucagon if on beta-blockers, ICU input. Vasopressin not in RCH algorithm.
proposed_fix: Either reframe correct option as 'PICU/anaesthetics review for advanced rescue; consider escalating adrenaline infusion and adding noradrenaline; vasopressin is a third-line ICU option' or add explicit RCH citation that this exceeds the published algorithm. Currently presents vasopressin as if it were RCH-standard.

issue: Correct option = IV cefotaxime plus ampicillin at meningitic doses. This is the AAP/US regimen. The AU pattern (RCH Febrile child + Therapeutic Guidelines + AMH) for an infant 1-3 months with meningitic features is benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) PLUS gentamicin PLUS cefotaxime; pure cefotaxime+ampicillin omits the aminoglycoside which AU guidelines include for sepsis cover and Listeria/enterococcal coverage.
rch_says: Empirical antibiotics for febrile infant 1-3 months guided by AU local protocols. RCH defers to Therapeutic Guidelines / local antimicrobial guidelines, which specify benzylpenicillin + gentamicin (+ cefotaxime if meningitis suspected) at this age.
proposed_fix: Change correct option to 'IV benzylpenicillin (or amoxicillin/ampicillin) PLUS gentamicin PLUS cefotaxime at meningitic doses' per AU/TG pattern. Add aciclovir if vesicles or focal seizure features per RCH HSV cover guidance.

issue: Correct option = 'IV ampicillin 50 mg/kg plus IV cefotaxime 50 mg/kg'. This is the AAP/Boston-criteria regimen. AU pattern is benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) PLUS gentamicin (+/- cefotaxime if meningitis). Omitting gentamicin loses Gram-negative coverage that AU guidelines favour for this age band.
rch_says: AU TG/AMH regimen for 1-3 mo febrile infant: benzylpenicillin + gentamicin; add cefotaxime if meningitis features. Cefotaxime + ampicillin alone is the US AAP regimen.
proposed_fix: Change correct option to AU regimen 'IV benzylpenicillin (or amoxicillin) PLUS gentamicin; add cefotaxime if meningitis suspected'. Keep current option as a US-derived distractor.

issue: Correct option uses 20 mL/kg as initial bolus, citing NSW Health Paediatric Sepsis Pathway. RCH and the most recent NSW paediatric sepsis pathway (2023-2024 update) actually recommend 10-20 mL/kg per bolus with reassessment after each, given post-FEAST evidence; the 20 mL/kg up-front number is still acceptable but newer practice favours 10 mL/kg with rapid reassessment. Also the ceftriaxone dose for suspected meningococcaemia is 100 mg/kg (max 4 g) under AU AMH; the option states 50 mg/kg.
rch_says: Sepsis bolus 10-20 mL/kg, reassess after each. Ceftriaxone meningitis/meningococcaemia dose is 100 mg/kg (max 4 g); 50 mg/kg is the standard non-meningitis sepsis dose.
proposed_fix: Change ceftriaxone dose to 100 mg/kg (max 4 g) for suspected meningococcaemia per AMH/AU. Soften 20 mL/kg first bolus to '10-20 mL/kg reassessed after each' per current AU pathway.
